parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected `dir` value to append a second statement or manipulate the `ORDER BY` expression.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can alter query logic or execute injected SQL depending on the database driver.

parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` and the generated query will include attacker-controlled SQL.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `dir` is interpolated directly into the `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF to reach internal admin or metadata endpoints, then pivot to further access.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can probe internal services or steal metadata from the server network.
parsed_findings.proof: req.query.url is passed directly to axios.get with no allowlist or URL validation.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:...` or a cloud metadata URL and the server will request it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: This endpoint fetches attacker-controlled URLs, creating server-side request forgery.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the intended restriction and use the unrestricted fetch to target internal hosts.
parsed_findings.impact: A broken filter leaves the fetch endpoint effectively unrestricted and amplifies the SSRF risk.
parsed_findings.proof: The condition `if (//api.partner.com'))` is syntactically broken and does not implement a valid URL restriction.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with any URL; the intended partner-domain filter is not enforced as written.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The target check is malformed and cannot reliably block partner API URLs.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the filter with repeated `../` segments, then read any accessible file outside the data root.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file read from the server filesystem.
parsed_findings.proof: `../../secret.txt` becomes `../secret.txt`, and `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolves outside the base dir.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../secret.txt` and read a file above `/srv/data` if it exists.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Single replace of '../' lets attackers keep traversal segments and escape `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a forged `state` cookie containing a malicious pickle gadget chain.
parsed_findings.impact: A remote attacker can execute code or trigger arbitrary object side effects on the server.
parsed_findings.proof: `request.cookies['state']` is attacker-controlled, and `pickle.loads(...)` will deserialize attacker-supplied opcodes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `state` to a base64-encoded malicious pickle payload and request `/resume`; the server will unpickle it before reading `last_page`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the path check with a crafted `src`, then inject shell metacharacters to run arbitrary commands.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands as the app user and exfiltrate or modify backup data.
parsed_findings.proof: A `name` like `x.tgz;id` or a `src` like `/var/data/app;id` becomes part of `cmd` and the shell runs the extra command.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON with `{"name":"x;id","src":"/var/data/app"}` or `{"src":"/var/data/app;id"}` and observe command execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`, enabling command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: No extra chain needed; the bug is directly exploitable through the transfer endpoint.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Tamper a valid JWT payload, keep the header and signature invalid, and rely on the production fallback to decode and trust it.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can impersonate any user or role by supplying a self-signed or modified token.
parsed_findings.proof: A forged JWT with any payload makes `jwt.verify` throw, then line 7 returns the decoded claims anyway.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `Authorization: Bearer <tampered JWT>` in production and observe `verifyToken()` returns its claims instead of rejecting it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, any token that fails signature verification is still accepted via `jwt.decode`, bypassing authentication.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use timing differences to recover the HMAC, then submit a matching X-Sig and trigger process_event().
parsed_findings.impact: A remote attacker may forge webhook requests without knowing SECRET.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can send many probes and measure response timing to gradually recover the expected hex digest.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send requests with increasingly correct X-Sig prefixes and compare latency across attempts.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The signature check uses a normal equality comparison, which can leak HMAC bytes via timing.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send malicious XML to `/xml/import`; the parser processes the entity during line 4 and returns parsed content.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read local files or trigger SSRF through entity resolution.
parsed_findings.proof: `resolve_entities=True` with `no_network=False` lets a crafted DOCTYPE load external entities during `fromstring(request.data, ...)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST XML containing a `<!DOCTYPE>` with an external entity to `/xml/import` and observe the parser fetch or expand it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The XML parser resolves external entities, allowing XXE when attacker-supplied XML is parsed.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use a known or guessed email, submit any password, and observe the login succeeding without real verification.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bypass authentication and gain access as any account they can match by email.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the fetched response to probe internal ports, then target sensitive internal admin or metadata endpoints.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can reach internal services and metadata endpoints from the server network context.
parsed_findings.proof: The only guard is `url.startswith('http')`, so `http://127.0.0.1` or `http://169.254.169.254` passes and is fetched by `requests.get`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow `buf` to smash saved frame data and redirect control flow.
parsed_findings.impact: This can crash the program and may enable code execution via stack corruption.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets` reads until newline without checking size, so input longer than 63 bytes overwrites adjacent stack data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and paste over 64 bytes before Enter to corrupt the stack.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer with unbounded input.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the recovered token in authenticated API requests to access the victim account.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass a NULL output buffer, skip the bad guard, then crash on the first write.
parsed_findings.impact: A caller can trigger a denial of service via NULL-pointer dereference.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` returns only when `out` is non-NULL, letting `out == NULL` reach the write path.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 8)` and it dereferences `out` at `memcpy` or `out[n]`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard is inverted, so valid `out` pointers are rejected and a NULL `out` reaches `memcpy` and `out[n]`.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"The guard is inverted, so valid `out` pointers are rejected and a NULL `out` reaches `memcpy` and `out[n]`.","proof":"`if (out || out_sz == 0)` returns only when `out` is non-NULL, letting `out == NULL` reach the write path.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(\"A\", NULL, 8)` and it dereferences `out` at `memcpy` or `out[n]`.","impact":"A caller can trigger a denial of service via NULL-pointer dereference.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":"Pass a NULL output buffer, skip the bad guard, then crash on the first write."}]}
